resulting from coexistent diseases, such as ischemia and arthritis, 
commonly coexist. Their effective rehabilitation requires thought-
ful attention to cancer-specific concerns. These may include life 
expectancy, individualized goals, symptom burden, and other 
treatment-related side effects. All these factors need to be consid -
ered when crafting compassionate and individualized treatment 
plans. This chapter provides an understanding of key issues rele -
vant to the rehabilitation of cancer survivors, with a particular 
focus on impairments related to a nervous system and/or muscu -
loskeletal etiology. This will include a brief introduction and 
overview of cancer treatment modalities, followed by reviewing 
common symptoms experienced by cancer survivors, highlighting 
specific populations of cancer survivors, and concluding with  
a review of precautions to ensure safety with the rehabilitation 
approach.
Epidemiology
Cancer is a common condition, impacting millions of Americans, 
and the number of cancer survivors is projected to reach 26 million 
by 2040. 165 Breast cancer is the leading cancer diagnosis in 
females and prostate cancer in males. These are followed by lung 
cancer and then colorectal cancer for both sexes. 4 The leading  
sites of new cancer diagnosis and death by sex are highlighted in 
Table 30.1.
Introduction
The prevalence of cancer survivors has increased over the past 
decades and is anticipated to continue to increase ( Fig. 30.1).165 
The National Cancer Institute categorizes any living individual 
who has been diagnosed with cancer, excluding skin cancers, as a 
cancer survivor. The majority of this large and growing popula -
tion of cancer survivors will experience symptoms or functional 
impairments related to their cancer and/or its treatment, which 
can deleteriously impact their function and quality of life. 118 The 
ramifications of cancer and its treatment can be long term and 
progressive. This expanding population highlights the need for 
the increasing awareness and expertise of physiatrists to provide 
specialized rehabilitation care for cancer survivors to optimize 
their function and quality of life.
Cancer rehabilitation aims to help cancer survivors regain their 
highest possible level of functionality and quality of life. 83 A suc-
cinct definition of the scope of cancer rehabilitation is: “Cancer 
rehabilitation is medical care that should be integrated throughout 
the oncology care continuum and delivered by trained rehabilita-
tion professionals who have it within their scope of practice to 
diagnose and treat patients’ physical, psychological, and cognitive 
impairments in an effort to maintain or restore function, reduce 
symptom burden, maximize independence, and improve quality of 
life in this medically complex population.”141 In addition to direct 
effect from cancer and its treatment, additional impairments  
30
Cancer Rehabilitation
CRISTINA KLINE-QUIROZ AND NOBLE G. JONES
• Cancer-related cognitive impairment is common during active 
cancer treatment and characterized by difficulties with 
executive function, concentration, memory, processing speed, 
and short-term memory.
• Lymphedema results in localized swelling that can cause 
physical symptoms, functional limitations, and negative impact 
on quality of life.
• Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy is a common 
adverse effect resulting from injury to peripheral nerves during 
cancer treatment.
• The treatment of bone metastases can include medication, 
radiation therapy, and/or surgical interventions, and 
rehabilitation can be beneficial but requires precautions.
• Radiation therapy is a common treatment for cancer; however, 
both the tumor and the surrounding healthy tissue can sustain 
damage from the radiation, leading to both direct and indirect 
injury, including late-effect radiation fibrosis syndrome.
• Chemotherapy agents can cause significant normal tissue 
toxicity and complications, such as peripheral neuropathy, 
cognitive dysfunction, and cardiotoxicity.
• Aromatase inhibitors used in adjuvant endocrine therapy can 
cause side effects, including osteoporosis, increased fracture 
risk, weight gain, and various musculoskeletal symptoms.
• Cancer-related fatigue is a common symptom affecting patients 
at all stages of their disease and can persist for months to years, 
significantly impacting function and quality of life. Despite its 
high prevalence, it is often underrecognized and undertreated.
SUMMARY PEARLS

Disparities
Disparities are present within cancer incidence, treatment, and 
survival. Patients with lower socioeconomic status (SES) have a 
higher incidence as well as higher likelihood of dying.165 This was 
particularly marked for lung, colorectal, cervical, stomach, and 
liver cancer.
142 Even after adjusting for poverty rate, non-Hispanic 
White individuals have a higher 5-year survival rate compared to 
Black, American Indian/Alaskan Native, and Asian/Pacific  
Islander males, as well as Black and American Indian/Alaskan  
Native females.
175 The more recent rates by race and ethnicity 
reported by the American Cancer Society are noted in Table 30.2. 
There are potential ways to decrease cancer disparities by focusing 
on prevention methods, such as reducing tobacco use, physical

inactivity, and obesity. Additionally, enhancing early detection, 
such as mammography, colorectal screening, and Pap tests, as well 
as effective treatment and palliative care, can also contribute to 
reducing these disparities.
175
Disparities within rehabilitation care are also evident across 
various demographic factors, including age, sex, geographic loca -
tion, SES, and insurance. Age-related disparities are notable, with 
older patients receiving less rehabilitation, with shorter inpatient 
rehabilitation admissions, despite their greater need.
125 Sex dispari-
ties are also significant. Research indicates that females are less likely 
to be referred for rehabilitation services compared to males. For 
instance, females are less likely to receive referrals for cardiac and 
stroke rehabilitation, resulting in less functional progress for these 
patients.
52,92 Geographic disparities further complicate access  
to rehabilitation services. Patients in rural areas face challenges in 
securing outpatient therapies due to a lack of nearby facilities, lead-
ing to reduced or nonexistent rehabilitation postdischarge.
57,113,150 
Economic disparities also significantly hinder cancer rehabilitation 
efforts, as patients with lower SES often face marginally covered or 
uncovered costs for essential items, such as compression garments, 
high copayments for physical and occupational therapy, and reduced 
home therapy benefits.
27,144,146
Health disparities are evident in both cancer care and rehabili-
tation care. Underrepresented minorities, individuals with lower 
SES, and those lacking insurance tend to experience higher rates 
of advanced disease and poorer outcomes in cancer treatment (see 
Table 30.2). Consequently, they may have an even greater need 
for rehabilitation services. However, their access to necessary reha-
bilitation care may also be hindered, compounding their overall 
health care challenges. Further study is needed to investigate the 
emerging disparities within cancer rehabilitation.
27
Staging
Cancer staging varies by cancer type but follows a general format 
to describe the spread of the disease from its origin. Commonly 
utilized is the T , N, and M system: T describes the characteristics 
of the primary tumor, N indicates the extent of regional lymph 
node spread, and M denotes the presence of distant metastases. 
Based on the TNM status, the disease is assigned a stage from I to

IV . Stage I represents early, localized disease, and stage IV indi-
cates advanced disease characterized by distant metastases, often 
considered incurable. Some cancers, such as primary brain  
tumors, are graded based on the degree of atypical cells observed 
histopathologically. Cancer may also be classified as in situ, local, 
regional, or distant, indicating whether it has remained in the 
layer of cells where it developed (in situ) or has spread beyond the 
tissue layer (local). The stage may direct treatment decisions and 
survival (Table 30.3). This information is also important for the 
physiatrist because it may influence selection of rehabilitation 
interventions and goals of care discussions with patients. Stage 
may also impact risk of recurrence or progression and guide ad -
ditional interdisciplinary collaboration with the oncology care 
team when there is concern for potential progression of disease or 
recurrence.
Prognosis
The complexity of cancer presents a challenge to patients and 
clinicians because there are various treatment approaches, patterns 
of metastatic spread, and prognosis. Considerations may include 
likelihood of progression based on cancer type and stage, available 
treatment options, potential toxicities from these treatment  
options, and patient comorbid conditions. Additionally, treatment 
options are evolving as research continues to drive the field for -
ward. Nonetheless, for the physiatrist and the individual patient, 
having some guidance regarding prognosis and expectations for 
disease course facilitates engagement in meaningful conversation 
regarding goals of care and treatment strategies. Table 30.3 shows 
the 5-year widely variable relative survival rates between 2013 and 
2019 for various cancers, highlighting the implications of regional 
and distant spread at the time of diagnosis.
4 Patients with prostate 
cancer have an excellent prognosis when detected early, but lung

cancer patients have a poorer prognosis, with a low 5-year survival 
rate. This information can inform rehabilitation goal setting, 
treatment approaches, and expectations. Understanding patterns 
of metastatic spread can also aid clinicians in directing their search 
for metastases because different cancers commonly spread to  
specific sites. For example, lung, breast, colon, and melanoma 
cancers commonly spread to the brain, highlighting the impor-
tance of neurologic screening exams; prostate, breast, lung, renal, 
and thyroid cancers commonly produce bone metastases. An  
online tool available for health care professionals to approximate 
survival is available at https://cancersurvivalrates.com. Questions 
to spur discussion among the interdisciplinary cancer and reha -
bilitation teams have been published: “Would you be surprised if 
this patient died in the next year?” or “Would you be surprised if 
this patient died in the next month?”
158 Additional predictors of 
survival include performance status, dyspnea, dysphagia, weight 
loss, xerostomia, anorexia, and cognitive impairment.
172
Stages of Cancer Rehabilitation
Dietz outlined four stages of rehabilitation goals: preventative,  
restorative, supportive, and palliative39 (Table 30.4). This framework 
helps to establish meaningful goals, informed by cancer prognosis 
and treatment. They facilitate a person-centered rehabilitation 
treatment plan aimed at enhancing quality of life and potential 
future rehabilitation needs.
Cancer Treatments
Cancer treatments vary widely depending on numerous factors, 
including cancer disease type, stage, patient comorbidities, goals  
of care, and more. T reatment may include surgery, chemotherapy, 
radiation therapy, hormonal therapy, or targeted therapies potentially

as a single modality or part of a multimodal treatment strategy. 
Each of these may have potential side effects (Table 30.5).
Surgery
Impairments resulting from surgery are influenced by the size, 
location, and type of the tumor. Healthy tissue may be impacted 
by surgical procedures aimed at local cancer control. The main 
reasons for removing normal tissue, despite the associated risk of 
long-term negative effects, include accurate staging (such as sam -
pling of lymph nodes or peritoneum), definitive removal of the 
tumor, ensuring local disease control (removal of lymph nodes 
that potentially contain cancer cells), and tissue harvesting for 
reconstruction. For the physiatrist, additional emphasis on the 
specific tissues and their potential functional ramifications is  
important, such as surgical interventions that may impact bone, 
nerve, muscle, lung parenchyma, and lymphatics. It is crucial to 
review patients’ surgical reports to be aware of known sacrifice  
or potential compromise to the neuromuscular and lymphatic 
systems. Furthermore, normal postoperative healing may be  
impacted by administration of additional anticancer treatments, 
such as radiation therapy and/or chemotherapy.
There is a vast array of established surgical methods for tumor 
eradication. Operations that often require the attention of a physical 
medicine and rehabilitation specialist due to the expected sequelae 
impacting function include neck dissection for oropharyngeal carci-
nomas, limb salvage or amputation for osteosarcoma, resection of 
truncal or limb myosarcoma, axillary lymph node dissection, and 
pneumonectomy or lobectomy for lung neoplasms. Other proce-
dures, such as nephrectomy, colectomy, mastectomy, and oophorec-
tomy, may involve the resection of muscles, nerves, and/or vessels to 
achieve clean margins, which can cause acute functional losses. 
Physiatric involvement and rehabilitation care are crucial when there 
is surgical resection of central and/or peripheral nervous system  
tissues due to the potential for neuromuscular sequelae.
In addition to the primary impairments noted, secondary im -
pairments can develop. These may result from altered biomechanics

Examples of this may include a secondary rotator cuff tendinopathy

Chemotherapy
Chemotherapy is a common modality utilized in cancer treatment in 
both curative intent and palliative settings. Neoadjuvant therapy is 
used before surgery to reduce tumor size. Induction chemotherapy,

A vast array of chemotherapeutic agents is used in oncologic 
practice. These drugs can be grouped into subclasses, including 
alkylating agents, platinum-based agents and their analogs, anti-
metabolites, topoisomerase inhibitor agents, antimicrotubule 
agents, differentiation agents, and miscellaneous agents. A nonex-
haustive summary of these agents and their potential side effects 
are highlighted in Table 30.6. The type, dose, and duration of

chemotherapy vary across cancer types and stages, but common 
strategies involve combining agents from different classes to  
exploit complementary mechanisms of action, achieve synergy, 
and reduce normal tissue toxicity. Examples of common chemo -
therapy regimens include CHOP (cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, 
vincristine, and prednisone), MOPP (mechlorethamine, vincristine, 
procarbazine, and prednisone), and AC-T (doxorubicin, cyclo -
phosphamide, paclitaxel).
16
Antineoplastic agents preferentially injure rapidly dividing 
cancer cells while relatively sparing normal cells. There can still be 
significant normal tissue toxicity, particularly in the skin and  
gastrointestinal systems. Common chemotherapeutic toxicities 
include peripheral neuropathy, cognitive dysfunction, cardiotox-
icity, and pulmonary fibrosis. Proactive screening has reduced the 
incidence of significant cardiopulmonary toxicity. Risk factors for 
bleomycin-induced acute pneumonitis include cumulative dose, 
age, smoking, renal dysfunction, mediastinal radiation therapy, 
and oxygen administration. Doxorubicin-associated cardiac toxic-
ity risk increases with cumulative doses.
17 Chemotherapy-induced 
peripheral neuropathy (CIPN) is a prevalent complication, with 
drugs such as vinca alkaloids, cisplatin, ixabepilone, taxanes, and 
thalidomide causing peripheral neurotoxicity.
143 Symptoms, pre-
sentation, and severity vary and will be reviewed in more detail in 
the CIPN section.
Radiation Therapy
Approximately 50% of all cancer survivors will have received  
radiation therapy during their treatment course. Radiation therapy 
uses ionizing radiation, primarily in the form of photons or  
particles to kill cancer cells. Photons, such as x-rays or gamma 
rays, or high-energy particles, such as protons, neutrons, and 
electrons, treat tumors by damaging the DNA of cancer cells and 
disrupting the cellular replication process with the goal of trigger-
ing cell death within the tumor. Radiation is delivered to the  
tumor via external beam radiation therapy outside the body or 
from within the body via brachytherapy.
9,14,18 Unfortunately, the 
tissue in proximity of the tumor experiences both direct and indi-
rect radiation injury. Like the tumor, healthy tissue within the 
radiation field receives direct injury causing DNA damage and 
triggering cell death. Indirect radiation injury is due to the forma-
tion of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species through the interac-
tion of ionizing radiation with water molecules to form reactive 
oxygen species and with endogenous nitric oxide to form reactive 
nitrogen species. These molecular moieties injure healthy cells, 
triggering an inflammatory response, leading to recruitment of 
fibroblasts, which then deposit extracellular matrix components 
into the surrounding tissue, resulting in radiation-induced fibrosis 
(RIF)
154,164,167 (Fig. 30.2). These radiation effects are classified as 
acute (occurring during or immediately after completion of treat-
ment), early delayed (occurring #3 months following completion 
of radiation treatment), and late delayed (occurring .3 months 
following the completion of radiation treatment). Early effects are 
usually temporary and affect rapidly proliferating tissues such as 
skin and mucosa. Late effects are often irreversible and impact 
tissues with low proliferative potential, such as connective tissue 
or neural tissue, with mechanisms involving microvascular dam-
age, cellular apoptosis, and endothelial changes.
119
Radiation fibrosis syndrome (RFS) is typically a late complica-
tion of radiation therapy, often manifesting years after treatment

pathologic process with symptoms that change over time due to 
the deterioration of affected structures. This sclerosing disease 
process can adversely affect any tissue type, including skin, muscu-

muscular, musculoskeletal, vascular, and/or organ system, resulting 
in related clinical manifestations and complications defined as RFS 
(Table 30.7). The symptoms patients may experience will depend 
heavily on the radiosensitivity of the tissues, organs, and structures 
exposed in the radiation field, radiation total dose, dose per frac -
tion, treated tissue volume, and treatment duration. Additionally, 
concurrent chemotherapy, prior or subsequent surgery, preexisting 
connective tissue diseases, and certain genetic predispositions con-
tribute to a higher likelihood of developing RFS.
155
RIF can cause peripheral or central nervous system (CNS) 
dysfunction through fibrotic- and stenotic-induced ischemic 
changes within the vaso vasorum and compressive and traction 
forces of soft tissues surrounding the nerves. These changes cause 
demyelination and axonal loss.
62,108 This is commonly seen in 
conditions such as radiation-induced cervical and brachial plexop-
athy, a common side effect of radiation treatment in patients with 
a history of head and neck cancer. Additionally, these neurologic 
changes may also present as autonomic disfunction (e.g., ortho -
static hypotension, baroreflex failure, sexual dysfunction, bladder 
dysfunction, and arrythmias).
124,156
Neuropathic pain, which arises from abnormal ectopic activity 
in neural structures that transmit pain signals to the spinal cord, 
thalamus, and brain, is common in RFS. It is typically out of pro-
portion to the perceived pathology. RIF can lead to the formation 
of myopathic muscles. Patients typically experience weakness,  
intermittent spasms, and pain, which is often exacerbated by radi-
ation-induced neurologic dysfunction. The progressive fibrotic 
process also results in a loss of elasticity in tendons and ligaments, 
thus adversely affecting mobility of joints involved in the field  
of radiation. In many cases, joint injury may be indirect due to 
neuromuscular changes rather than direct injury to the joint. Most 
patients experience acute radiation-induced dermatitis, which ini-
tially improves but is later followed by chronic radiation-induced 
dermatitis. Symptoms include poor wound healing, changes in 
skin appearance and texture, necrosis, and ulcerations. Radiation 
effects on bone can result in accelerated osteoporosis, development 
of osteonecrosis, abnormal bone growth, and decrease in hemato-
poietic function.
78,156 RIF is a significant contributor to the devel-
opment of lymphedema, especially in the setting of lymph node 
dissection. This can affect any part of the body where lymph nodes 
have been exposed, such as the neck, breast, trunk, pelvic region, 
and upper and lower extremities. RFS can cause microvascular 
injury and can lead to accelerated atherosclerosis, obliterative end-
arteritis, peripheral vascular disease, venoocclusive disease, throm-
boembolism, and venous insufficiency. These vascular changes also 
result in worsening ischemic changes to the tissue and organs 
supplied by the affected blood vessels.
122,184
Many patients experience neuromuscular system, autonomic 
system, and organ system dysfunction as a result of RFS (e.g., 
Hodgkin lymphoma patients typically experience endocrine dys-
function [hypothyroidism], neuromuscular dysfunction [cervical-
gia, cervical plexopathy, brachial plexopathy, scapular winging], 
musculoskeletal dysfunction [dropped head syndrome, cervicalgia], 
pulmonary dysfunction [radiation-induced pulmonary fibrosis of 
the lungs], cardiovascular dysfunction [baroreceptor reflex failure, 
cardiac valvular dysfunction, cardiac fibrosis], and gastrointestinal 
dysfunction [dysphagia] due to mantle field radiation treatment). 
RFS symptoms in breast cancer patients typically include lymph-
edema, adhesive capsulitis, and brachial plexopathy. T o minimize 
adverse radiation effects, radiation oncologists attempt to focus the 
radiation on tumors while protecting surrounding healthy tissues.
Radiation oncologists must carefully evaluate multiple factors 
when planning treatment, including tissue radiosensitivity, irradi-
ated volume, radiation dose, and fractionation schedule. Radiation 
dose is measured in grays, with patients feeling no pain during 
treatment due to the nature of ionizing energy and lack of heat 
generation. T o minimize side effects on healthy cells, radiation 
oncologists utilize a variety of dose delivery techniques to concen-
trate the radiation administered on tumors while limiting exposure 
to surrounding tissues. When developing a treatment plan, radia-
tion oncologists also consider whether treatment is intended to be 
curative or palliative, and the patient’s ability to tolerate radiation 
treatment, because this influences the acceptable level of toxicity. 
This comprehensive assessment is crucial for balancing potential 
toxicities against the likelihood of therapeutic benefits and long-
term quality of life for each patient.
10,60,70
Hormone or Endocrine Therapies
Hormone or endocrine therapies are systemic treatments utilized 
to treat malignancies whose growth is promoted by hormones. 
These are most commonly employed as part of the treatment for 
breast and prostate cancer, but they have additional indications 
(endometrial, ovarian, and adrenal cancers).
Tamoxifen is a selective estrogen receptor modulator that is 
utilized in the treatment of hormone receptor positive breast cancer,

cancer.13,101 It functions by competitively binding to estrogen  
receptors on tumor cells and other organ tissues, acting as an anti-
estrogen in the breast tissue but a weak estrogen agonist in the  
endometrium. Notable risk factors associated with tamoxifen  
include an increased risk of stroke, pulmonary embolism, endome-
trial adenocarcinoma, and uterine sarcoma.49 The impact of tamox-
ifen on bone varies based on menstrual status. It is associated with 
protective benefits on bone mineral density in postmenopausal  
females but a decline in bone mineral density in premenopausal 
females. Side effects include musculoskeletal pain with arthropathy 
and myalgia, although at lower rates that aromatase inhibitors.106
Aromatase inhibitors (AIs) inhibit the enzyme aromatase, 
which decreases plasma estrogen levels (estrone, estradiol, estrone 
sulfate). Medications in use include anastrozole, exemestane, and 
letrozole. These are commonly prescribed as adjuvant endocrine 
therapy for postmenopausal hormone receptor–positive breast 
cancer or premenopausal breast cancer in conjunction with ovarian 
function suppression. Additionally, some are utilized for recurrent 
ovarian cancer.36,127 Common side effects include osteoporosis, 
increased fracture risk, increased incidence of ischemic heart dis -
ease, weight gain, edema, nausea, fatigue, depression, hot flashes, 
and night sweats. Of particular note for rehabilitation providers is 
the high frequency of musculoskeletal side effects, known as aro -
matase inhibitor-induced musculoskeletal symptoms (AIMSS). 
AIMSS is a common sequelae impacting 47% to 82%, which can 
lead to discontinuation of therapy in nearly one-fourth of 
cases.12,32,95 Therefore it is crucial to address these symptoms to 
ensure that patients can continue their treatment. The approach to 
managing AIMSS varies depending on whether the symptoms 
present more focally as arthralgias or tendinopathies or more  
generalized diffuse pain. Focal symptoms can be treated based on 
the rehabilitation strategies utilized for their etiologies unrelated to 
AIs. Diffuse symptoms require care for the entire body. Participating 
in 150 minutes of aerobic exercise per week and strength training 
has been shown to decrease pain. 74 Yoga and acupuncture were 
found to improve symptoms in small studies. 31,54 Supplements 
such as glucosamine (1500 mg/day) with chondroitin sulfate 
(1200 mg/day) has shown moderate pain improvement.58 Vitamin 
D has mixed results in studies.128,138 Pharmacologic options include 
nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) and acetamino -
phen used empirically.83 Duloxetine was shown to have improve -
ment in pain related to AIMSS in a randomized controlled trial.67
Luteinizing hormone-releasing hormone (LHRH) agonists, 
also known as LHRH analogs or gonadotropin-releasing hor -
mone agonists, are medications that reduce the production of 
testosterone. These include goserelin, leuprolide, and triptorelin.115 
Antiandrogen medications or androgen deprivation therapies  
include apalutamide, enzalutamide, darolutamide, bicalutamide, 
flutamide, and nilutamide. 115 These medications bind to andro -
gen receptors, preventing androgens from promoting tumor 
growth. Side effects may include osteoporosis, loss of muscle 
mass, weight gain, and fatigue.
Targeted Therapies
Targeted therapies are therapeutic agents that are specifically de -
signed to target small molecules responsible for the proliferation of 
cancer. There are several types of targeted therapies, which include 
angiogenesis inhibitors (bevacizumab), monoclonal antibodies 
(trastuzumab, cetuximab), proteasome inhibitors (bortezomib), 
and signal transduction inhibitors (imatinib). Please note this  
section only provides a brief overview and is not an all-encompassing 
review.
T rastuzumab, a recombinant humanized monoclonal antibody, 
is utilized in the treatment of HER2-positive breast, uterine, gastric, 
colorectal, and esophageal cancers. It downregulates proliferation of 
cells, which overexpress HER2. A notable potential adverse reaction 
is cardiomyopathy, decreased left ventricular ejection fraction, and 
arrhythmias. The cardiomyopathy is more prevalent in patients 
who also received an anthracycline.79
Bevacizumab, a recombinant humanized monoclonal anti -
body, inhibits the activity of vascular endothelial growth factor, a 
proangiogenic growth factor. It is utilized in treating metastatic 
colorectal cancer, non–small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), and 
breast and ovarian cancers. However, cardiotoxic effects, such as 
angina, heart failure, hypertension, stroke, myocardial infarction, 
and arterial thromboembolic events, are potential side effects. The 
cardiovascular risk is increased when used in conjunction with 
anthracycline. Patients older than 65 years of age or those with 
previous arterial thromboembolic events face a higher risk. 51,107
Tyrosine kinase inhibitors, including imatinib, nilotinib,  
dasatinib, and ponatinib, are commonly used and effective in the 
treatment of chronic myeloid leukemia. Potential side effects  
include fatigue, weight change, gastrointestinal symptoms, rash, 
cardiovascular disease, pulmonary disease, cytopenias, cognitive 
impairment, peripheral neuropathy, arthralgias, and myalgias.153
Immunotherapy
The utilization of immunotherapy has been advancing recently 
with the development of monoclonal antibodies to leverage the 
regulatory molecules of the immune response–immune checkpoint 
inhibitors. These novel medications include ipilimumab, pembroli-
zumab, nivolumab, and durvalumab. These can be used for  
advanced melanoma, renal cell carcinoma, NSCLC, bladder cancer, 
and lymphomas. Ipilimumab inhibits cytotoxic T-lymphocyte anti-
gen-4–enhancing immune response, enabling cytolytic T cells to 
identify and trigger apoptosis. Pembrolizumab and nivolumab are 
monoclonal antibodies that bind to the programmed cell death-1 
(PD-1) receptor to block the binding of its ligands, PDL-1 and 
PDL-2. By blocking PD-1, these treatments undermine the  
immune resistance strategies utilized by cancer cells. While these 
innovative immunotherapies are generally well tolerated, they can 
cause immune-related adverse events, including colitis/diarrhea, 
rash or dermatitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, and endocrinopathies. 
There are emerging data regarding immune-related neuromuscular 
events, which may be experienced by 1% to 3% of patients and 
include myasthenia gravis, myositis, acute inflammatory demyelin-
ating polyneuropathy, and chronic inflammatory demyelinating 
polyneuropathy.86,111
Common Conditions Related to  
Cancer and Cancer Treatments
Fatigue
Cancer-related fatigue is a common and distressing symptom  
affecting patients at all stages of their disease, from initial presen -
tation to survivorship, and can persist for months to years. It 
negatively impacts function and quality of life, with its prevalence 
reported to be as high as 70% to 99%. 43,56 However, this symp-
tom is often underrecognized and undertreated, with historically 
50% of patients not discussing treatment options for fatigue with

tiredness or exhaustion related to cancer or cancer treatment that

8 Patients may experience difficulty concentrating, 
emotional exhaustion, unrefreshing sleep, insomnia, or hyper-

43 It is crucial to acknowledge and treat fatigue to reduce

As part of the management strategy, it is important to evalu -
ate for and treat reversible sources of fatigue through laboratory 
testing or sleep studies. Potential reversable sources are listed in

identified etiology and may persist for years. The NCCN pub -

screening, evaluation, and treatment strategies, and they have 
also published a resource for patients.
116 Potential cooccurring  
deconditioning and mood disorders should also be addressed. 
There is evidence supporting recommendations for physical  
activity, yoga, massage therapy, cognitive behavioral therapy, 
and psychoeducational therapies.
76 Psychostimulants may also 
be considered, but further research is required. 76,109 For patients 
at the end of life, short-term use of corticosteroids has support 
to reduce fatigue, but long-term use is limited due side effects.

Cognitive Impairment
Cancer-related cognitive impairment (CRCI) is common dur-
ing active cancer treatment, impacting up to 75% of non-CNS 
cancers and 90% of CNS cancers.
3 Of cancer survivors, 30% to 
60% continue to experience cognitive symptoms even after 
treatment ends.
1,3,170 Although it is often colloquially referred 
to as “chemobrain,” it is not as accurate as CRCI because che -
motherapy is not the single contributor to the etiology of these 
symptoms; it can be present in cancer survivors who have not 
been treated with chemotherapeutic agents.
105 Individuals may 
experience difficulty with executive function, concentration, 
memory, processing speed, and short-term memory. This can be 
a major source of distress with deleterious impacts on school/
work performance, social functioning, and community reinte -
gration.
1,129,171 Several potential mechanisms may contribute to 
CRCI, including impaired blood-brain barrier integrity, cell 
death in the hippocampus and corpus collosum, genetic vari-
ability or polymorphisms, DNA damage, cytokine production, 
telomere shortening, defects in neural repair, hormone dysregu-
lation, and neurotransmitter defects.
19,38,48,75 Whole-brain  
radiation is also known to be neurotoxic. 19 Additional influ -
ences may include chronic stress, loneliness, medical comor -
bidities, hormonal changes, physical inactivity, depression, 
anxiety, insomnia, and side effects from other medications.
6,66,136
Evidence guiding management of CRCI is limited. Thus far no 
brief screening tool has been identified to be effective. 117 Neuro-
psychological testing helps assess alterations to specific functions; 
however, this requires specialized providers and significant time, 
limiting the ability to scale its use more broadly.
135 It may be ap-
propriate to pursue neuropsychological testing in some instances, 
such as cancer survivors with symptoms persisting for several 
months.
117 It is important to assess for potential reversible factors 
contributing to CRCI ( Box 30.2). Neuroimaging is utilized for 
patients presenting with focal neurologic deficits, especially for 
those with known CNS metastatic disease or for those who are at 
high risk for such metastases, to rule out structural abnormalities. 
It is important to provide patients with validation of their experience 
with CRCI by providing appropriate reassurance and support 
with self-management and coping strategies. First-line interventions 
include cognitive rehabilitation, psychotherapy, and physical  
activity. Additional nonpharmacologic recommendations include 
meditation, yoga, mindfulness-based stress reduction, and cogni -
tive training. Although evidence is limited, second-line treatment 
medications (methylphenidate, modafinil, donepezil) may be 
considered in selective cases.
117

85 
Lymphedema presents with localized swelling, depending on the 
location of lymphatic system disruption, such as arm swelling in 
breast cancer survivors. Head and neck cancer survivors may expe-
rience swelling externally with visible changes in the face and neck 
or internally impacting the aerodigestive tract. Of breast cancer 
survivors, 20% to 30% develop lymphedema.
34,145 For head and 
neck cancer survivors, newer studies have found a higher incidence 
up to 90% when evaluating for both internal and external 
lymphedema.
132 This localized swelling can detrimentally impact 
physical symptoms, functional limitations, and quality of life.37,65,68 
If lymphedema severity progresses, patients may develop skin 
changes, including hyperpigmentation, hyperkeratosis, papilloma-
tosis, and lymphorrhea. Before delving into lymphedema, it is 
important to note that not all limb swelling in cancer survivors is 
lymphedema. Box 30.3 lists potential causes of swelling in cancer 
survivors.
Lymphedema is categorized into stages, from 0 to 3. Stage 0 or 
1a, also known as the latency or subclinical stage, is when a  
patient is at risk of developing lymphedema due to underlying 
impaired lymph transport; however, there is no visible swelling at 
this stage. There may be subtle changes in tissues or subjective 
symptoms. Stage 1 is the spontaneous/reversible stage, character -
ized by reversible swelling that improves with elevation. The 
edema is soft and pitting. Stage 2 is spontaneously irreversible 
with harder swelling with increased limb volume that does not 
improve with elevation. Volume reductions are still possible with 
appropriate treatment. This is when patients begin to experience 
tissue fibrosis and skin thickening. Stage 3 is marked by skin 
changes (hyperpigmentation, hyperkeratosis, papillomatosis) and 
fibrosis, which is also referred to as lymphostatic elephantiasis
46 
(Fig. 30.3).
Early detection and intervention are essential. In addition to 
visible changes, lymphedema can also contribute to functional 
limitations due to pain, discomfort, and altered biomechanics. 
Individuals with lymphedema are at increased risk of developing 
cellulitis. In addition to physical symptoms, patients may experi -
ence psychosocial effects as changes in appearance can impact their 
quality of life and lead to higher levels of distress, anxiety, and 
depression.
7 Appropriate treatment of lymphedema can decrease 
swelling, lower cellulitis rates, and decrease hospitalizations.178
Evaluation of lymphedema may involve imaging and volume 
measurements. Lymphoscintigraphy, which involves injecting a 
radiolabeled tracer, aids in visualizing lymphatic dysfunction. 
Near-infrared fluorescence imaging with indocyanine green lym -
phography is also used to identify lymphatic dysfunction. T o 
monitor progression, several methods are routinely used for vol -
ume measurements, including circumferential tape measurements 
and perometry. Bioimpedance spectroscopy is increasingly being 
utilized to assess for subclinical lymphedema.
There is growing evidence to support a prospective surveillance 
model to reduce progression to and rates of chronic breast cancer–
related lymphedema, improving patient outcomes and reducing 
health care costs.
102,130,152 Recently, the NCCN survivorship 
guideline also added a specific inclusion of the use of bioimped-
ance spectroscopy when available. 137 In this model, routine sur -
veillance is conducted every 3 months for the first 3 years and 
every 6 months in years 4 and 5. If subclinical lymphedema is 
identified, patients initiate treatment with a compression garment 
of 23 to 32 mm Hg daily for 4 weeks. Should the patient exhibit

Standard of care for lymphedema management is CDT . 
Lymphedema therapy consists of two stages. The first component 
of CDT under guidance of a lymphedema therapist includes 
manual lymphatic drainage (MLD), short-stretch compressive 
bandaging, skin care, therapeutic exercise, and compression gar -
ments. The goal of the initial phase is to decrease lymphedema 
volume. This phase involves regular sessions of MLD with a 
lymphedema therapist, followed by compression bandaging and 
exercises. After maximum volume reduction is achieved, patients 
initiate a long-term maintenance program. In this phase, com -
pressive garments are worn during the day. MLD, also referred to 
as lymphatic massage, is a specialized technique to stimulate 
lymph vessels, leading to increased transport of the congested 
lymphatic system. MLD treatments start in lymphostatic regions 
adjacent to functional lymphotomes and gradually progress to 
distal regions (Fig. 30.4). Skin care is emphasized to maintain skin

Microvascular surgical techniques, such as lymphovenous  
bypass or lymphovenous anastomosis and vascularized lymph 
node transfer, to improve residual lymphatic function are becom-
ing more prevalent. Lymphovenous bypass is a procedure that 
establishes connections between lymphatic and venous microves -
sels. Vascularized lymph node transfer involves removing a lymph 
node or nodes from the omentum or an intact node bed and  
relocating it to the congested area. Although growing and evolv -
ing, the overall level of supportive evidence is still limited, but 
there may be potential utility in such procedures as a preventative 
intervention at the time of axillary lymph node dissection 
(ALND). Based on the safety profile and feasibility of lympho-
venous anastomosis, preliminary reports suggest that preventive 
lymphatic surgery may help prevent chronic cancer-related 
lymphedema. Nonetheless, a systematic review highlighted the 
lack of high-quality studies on preventive lymphatic surgery, lim-
iting recommendations for this approach.
26
Peripheral Neuropathy
CIPN is a condition resulting from damage to peripheral nerves. 
It can cause symptoms such as numbness, tingling, pain, weak -
ness, and gait abnormalities due to the involvement of motor and/or 
sensory nerves. Autonomic neuropathy is less common but may 
present with orthostatic hypotension, constipation, urinary diffi -
culties, or sexual dysfunction. These symptoms can interfere with 
daily activities and reduce quality of life. They may also limit the 
amount of chemotherapy a patient can tolerate. The incidence of 
this condition is dependent on several factors, including the che-
motherapy drug(s) used, dosage, patient’s comorbidities, and 
method of assessment. Drugs associated with neuropathy include 
taxanes, vinca alkaloids, platinum compounds, bortezomib, and 
thalidomide. These medications can cause different symptoms 
based on their mechanisms. For instance, vinca alkaloids and 
taxanes inhibit microtubules leading to axonal damage, present -
ing with a length-dependent distal symmetric motor and sensory 
neuropathy.
90 Symptoms may appear early in treatment and prog-
ress with dose and duration, typically improving after treatment 
completion.
163 In contrast, platinum compounds cause predomi-
nantly sensory symptoms by accumulating in the dorsal root 
ganglia and causing DNA damage resulting in symptoms that 
may present and worsen for months after treatment, known as a 
coasting phenomenon.
126,140 Of note, patients with Charcot-  
Marie-T ooth disease require special consideration because they are 
at risk of severe neuropathy following treatment with vincristine.
69
As part of evaluation and treatment of CIPN, a thorough patient 
history is vital, including details on symptoms, onset, timing in rela-
tion to treatment, distribution, severity, pain assessment, functional 
assessment, and the patient’s ability to carry out daily activities. Lab 
tests can help exclude other possible causes of neuropathy, such as 
paraneoplastic neurologic disorders or metabolic/nutritional defi-
ciencies. Refer to Box 30.4 for a broad but not all-encompassing list 
of potential etiologies for neuropathic symptoms. There are currently 
no approved treatments that effectively prevent CIPN. Regular 
monitoring during chemotherapy is recommended due to the po-
tential for symptom progression.
82 Pain and paresthesia manage -
ment strategies are often based on treatments proven effective for 
painful diabetic neuropathy, with duloxetine and pregabalin showing 
efficacy in studies specific to CIPN.
120,134,148 Other potential phar-
macologic options include gabapentin, venlafaxine, amitriptyline,

Pain
Pain is common for cancer survivors, impacting 66% of patients 
with advanced disease and 39% following curative intent treat -
ment.169 The etiology of this pain may be due to the direct  
involvement of the tumor, treatment effects, or factors unrelated 
to the cancer or its treatment, such as comorbid musculoskeletal 
conditions.20,84 Severe pain can negatively affect functionality and 
quality of life. Given that the cause of pain during cancer treat -
ment and survivorship can be due to various factors, it is crucial 
to identify the pain generator(s) to guide treatment approaches 
effectively. This can help reduce pain and optimize function and 
quality of life. Pain management strategies can include targeted 
therapy referrals, therapeutic exercise, modalities, oral and topical 
medications, and injection procedures. Studies have highlighted 
the positive impact of patient education on managing cancer 
pain. A review article emphasized key principles, including pain 
education, patient-centered assessment and management, the 
importance of patient empowerment, and the integral role of 
patient education in routine care. 97 A meta-analysis also found 
interventions that fostered “enablement” to be effective. 100 Pain 
may be intertwined with fear of cancer progression or recurrence. 
Therefore education about pain generators, pathophysiology, and 
treatment plans may help alleviate fear and enhance a sense of 
control. By providing education, there is enhanced potential for 
shared decision making within the range of treatment options.
Physical therapy and therapeutic exercise can help stabilize pain-
ful areas and alleviate myofascial pain.21 A systematic review focus-
ing on breast cancer survivors showed that physical therapy was 
effective in reducing pain and enhancing range of motion (ROM).59 
Isometric contractions that avoid pain-causing movements may be 
better tolerated.23 A systematic review found that exercise is benefi-
cial at all stages of cancer treatment, for all types of cancer, and can 
enhance physical function and alleviate impairments.151 Important 
safety considerations and precautions are essential to account for 
coexisting conditions, such bone metastases, peripheral neuropa -
thy, or cardiopulmonary dysfunction. This is reviewed later in this 
chapter.
Modalities may also be utilized to help ameliorate pain. These 
may include topical heat, cryotherapy, and transcutaneous electri-
cal nerve stimulation. Complementary and alternative medicine 
techniques, including acupuncture, cupping, music therapy, and 
reflexology, may also help alleviate cancer pain. However, more 
research is required to substantiate their benefits. The use of 
braces, orthotics, assistive devices, and compensatory strategies 
can also be beneficial in reducing pain and enhancing function.23 
Osteopathic manipulative treatment (OMT) outside the cancer 
population has shown benefit in addressing pain, and an emerg -
ing body of evidence also indicates that OMT may help lessen 
pain and enhance function for cancer survivors. With appropriate 
safety precautions, most OMT techniques may be used for cancer 
survivors, but more research is needed.103
There are numerous tools available to enhance physical mobil-
ity and function, including orthotics, braces, and assistive devices 
such as sock aids and long-handled reachers. For cancer survivors 
whose functional capacity is limited, these tools can be crucial in 
maintaining independence, which can in turn impact their men -
tal health and quality of life. 21 The provision of assistive devices 
and bracing depends on the individual patient. Offloading of 
painful joints such as with the use of a cane or walker can decrease 
pain. Bracing can also help optimize posture and alignment, such 
as using a Headmaster or Velpeau collar as needed for individuals 
with dropped head syndrome.81
There are many medications available to help ameliorate pain. 
These may include analgesics such as acetaminophen, NSAIDs, or 
opioids. The choice of medication depends on the pain generator. 
For instance, when pain is due to bone metastasis, NSAIDs, 
bisphosphonates, or radiotherapy may be helpful. 2 Other fre -
quently used medications for neuropathic pain include gabapentin, 
pregabalin duloxetine, venlafaxine, amitriptyline, nortriptyline, and 
topical capsaicin.82,153
Injection procedures may also be appropriate as part of the 
comprehensive approach to pain for cancer survivors. This may 
include corticosteroids, local anesthetics, and botulinum toxins. 
For myofascial pain, dry needling and trigger points may be uti -
lized.21 Corticosteroids can be utilized in a similar fashion as 
practiced in general rehabilitation but may require precautions 
with the oncology care team during active treatment particularly 
in cytopenic patients. Botulinum toxin injections can be utilized 
as part of the management of cervical dystonia related to late  
effects of radiation fibrosis or trismus as well as for specific etiolo-
gies of postmastectomy pain. 89,162 Additional injections utilized 
for postmastectomy pain include serratus plane blocks and inter -
costobrachial nerve blocks.179,185 These are just a few examples of 
the vast cadre of injection techniques that may be utilized.

der dysfunction, post–breast surgery pain syndrome, AIMSS,  
and lymphedema. They may also present with brachial plexopa -
thies, radiculopathies, and mononeuropathies. Fatigue, cognitive

were discussed earlier in this chapter. Postmastectomy pain syn-
drome, also known as post–breast surgery pain syndrome, is a 
chronic pain condition that can occur after any breast surgery.
174 
Its prevalence ranges widely, but may be up to 68%. 77,98 This 
syndrome encompasses a variety of diagnostic subgroups that can 
stem from different causes, including shoulder dysfunction, chest 
wall pain, radiation fibrosis, axillary web syndrome, intercostobra-
chial neuralgia, and phantom breast pain. Identifying the specific 
cause of pain is crucial to inform the appropriate treatment, 
which can include physical therapy, pharmacotherapy, and injec-
tion procedures. Physical therapy is a cornerstone of management 
and may include therapeutic exercise, neuromuscular reeduca -
tion, progressive resistance training, desensitization techniques, 
and myofascial release. Medication options include NSAIDs,  
gabapentin, pregabalin, and duloxetine depending on the pain 
generator (e.g., a short course of NSAIDs for pain related to a 
rotator cuff tendinopathy or gabapentin for neuropathic pain).
5,88 
Depending on the cause of the pain, procedures may include  
intercostobrachial nerve block, serratus plane block, or botulinum 
toxin injections.
88,179
Breast cancer survivors can experience shoulder dysfunction, 
characterized by pain, restricted ROM, stiffness, weakness, and 
impaired function, often due to rotator cuff pathology and adhesive 
capsulitis. This is often grouped together as post–breast surgery 
pain but highlights the importance of identifying the pain genera-
tor to tailor treatment appropriately. Not only does shoulder mor-
bidity affect the shoulder ipsilateral to the breast cancer, but the 
contralateral shoulder can also be negatively impacted, exhibiting 
altered kinematics and muscle activity compared to healthy indi-
viduals. Surgery and radiation fibrosis can impact shoulder posi-
tioning and function due to guarding of the shoulder movements 
after treatment as well as pectoralis muscle hypertonicity and short-
ening contributing to altered biomechanics.
24,42 Additional factors 
may include axillary web syndrome (Fig. 30.5) and lymph-
edema.
68,166 Physical therapy is a crucial part of treatment, with a 
Cochrane review recommending prompt postoperative implemen-
tation due to significant improvements in shoulder ROM.
161,163

NSAIDs can help manage mild to moderate pain, and corticosteroid 
injections may provide temporary relief.
24,159
AIMSS may affect as many as 82% of females taking AIs for 
hormone-sensitive breast cancer and can lead to discontinuation 
of therapy in 24% of cases.
32,93,95 Therefore addressing these 
musculoskeletal complaints is crucial to help support continued 
use of AIs. T reatment approaches vary based on whether the 
symptoms are focal arthralgias or tendinopathies vs. diffuse pain. 
Focal symptoms can be managed based on their standard treat-
ment outside AIMSS, while care for diffuse symptoms focuses on 
the entire body. Among breast cancer survivors with AIMSS, 
aerobic exercise and supervised strength training have been 
found to decrease pain.
74 NSAIDs and acetaminophen have been 
used empirically. A randomized controlled trial found that  
duloxetine reduced pain in AIMSS. 67 Some supplements, such  
as glucosamine chondroitin, have shown improvements, and  
vitamin D with mixed results in pain and function was reported 
in certain studies.
128 Additionally, small studies have found that 
acupuncture and yoga can help improve AIMSS. 31,54
Head and Neck Cancer
Head and neck cancer is a diverse group of malignancies that can 
affect various areas, including the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, sali-
vary glands, and paranasal sinuses. It is the seventh most common 
cancer worldwide, and it is commonly diagnosed at an advanced 
stage, often requiring multimodal treatment.
11,25 Survivors of head 
and neck cancer may experience a range of neuromusculoskeletal 
disorders, such as shoulder dysfunction, spinal accessory nerve 
palsy, cervical dystonia, dysphagia, dysarthria, trismus, lymph -
edema, and other conditions.
121
Shoulder dysfunction is prevalent among head and neck can -
cer survivors, with reports of loss of ROM in abduction and pain 
as high as 100%.
55 In a small study, there were electrodiagnostic 
findings of spinal accessory nerve impairment in all cases of neck 
dissection, even when the spinal accessory nerve was intentionally 
spared.
45 Additionally, cervical nerve roots and the brachial plexus 
may be in the radiation field, resulting in weakness of the rotator 
cuff muscles.
154 This contributed to abnormal scapular and gleno-
humeral motion, negatively impacting shoulder function and 
pain.
72 Fig. 30.6 shows an example of scapular dyskinesia. Rotator 
cuff tendinopathies and adhesive capsulitis are common.83
The majority of head and neck cancer survivors experience 
neck dysfunction, with neck pain and decreased ROM. Cervical 
dystonia can be caused by injury to neuromuscular structures 
(spinal accessory nerve, cervical nerve roots, cervical plexus) and 
radiation fibrosis.
154 Patients may experience pain, tightness, and 
spasms in the neck muscles, particularly the sternocleidomastoid, 
scalene complex, and trapezius. T reatment options can include 
physical therapy with a lifelong home exercise program, medica-
tions for pain such as gabapentin or pregabalin, and botulinum 
toxin injections.
28,154 Of note, appropriate muscle selection for 
botulinum toxin is key, and it is generally advised to avoid the 
posterior cervicothoracic musculature in this population due to 
the increased risk of overweakening, leading to dropped head 
syndrome.
Dropped head syndrome is characterized by weakness and atro-
phy of the cervicothoracic muscle leading to difficulty holding the 
head in a neutral position
64,154 (Fig. 30.7). The prevalence of this 
for head and neck cancer survivors remains unknown. Although 
not needed for diagnosis, electrodiagnostic testing can reveal neuro-
pathic, myopathic, or mixed results.

to address pain. Botulinum toxin injections into select muscles, 
including masseters and temporalis, can decrease pain and

Lymphedema within this population was historically underdiag-

Swelling in this body region can also be internal, resulting in

Functional implications include potential detrimental effects on 
swallowing, voice, cervical ROM, body image, a nd overall qual-

considered.

Brain Tumors
Brain tumors may be primary, originating from various cells 
within the CNS, or from other primary cancers that have metas -
tasized to the CNS. Cancers that commonly spread to the CNS 
include lung, breast, colorectal, melanoma, and genitourinary 
cancers. Brain metastases are more prevalent than primary brain 
tumors, with an estimated 70,000 to 400,000 new cases annually 
in the United States.
87 Primary brain tumors make up less than 
2% of all new cancer diagnoses, with the 2024 estimated number 
of new cases for brain and other CNS at 25,400; however, they 
cause significant disability and account disproportionately for 
morbidity and mortality.
4
Individuals with brain tumors may experience wide ranging 
neurologic and functional deficits. Their presentation may vary 
depending on the size and location of the tumor. These functional 
impairments can be a direct result of the tumor itself, such as tissue 
destruction, compression of normal brain tissue, and increased 
intracranial pressure. They can also stem from the side effects of 
treatments, including tissue loss following surgery, steroid myopathy, 
and the acute or late effects from radiation therapy and chemo -
therapy. Potential symptoms include headache, mental disturbance, 
focal weakness, seizures, speech difficulty, visual disturbance, sensory 
disturbance, and gait or limb ataxia.
73,168
Advancements in surgical methods, chemotherapy, radiation 
therapy, and immunotherapy have extended survival time for indi-
viduals with brain tumors. 71 Corticosteroids are commonly uti -
lized in this population to reduce peritumor edema and brain 
compression. Given that brain tumors can cause considerable 
neurologic and functional impairments, it is crucial for patients to 
receive rehabilitation services to enhance their function and quality 
of life. A study revealed that over 80% of patients with CNS  
tumors display needs for rehabilitation, which were commonly 
multifactorial and included cognitive impairment, visual-perceptual

tailored to the individual’s presentation, with goals akin to those 
for other brain disorders (e.g., optimizing functional transfers, bed 
mobility, ambulation, wheelchair training, family training). Equip-

common in individuals with brain tumors, significantly disrupting

and has been shown to enhance attention and verbal memory 
while also reducing mental fatigue, ultimately aiding in the social 
and professional reintegration of patients.121
Spinal Cord Tumors
Spinal cord tumors are categorized based on their anatomic loca -
tion, either as extradural or intradural, with similar prevalence 
rates.121 Most extradural tumors are metastatic lesions, which  
are more common than primary spine tumors. Within the spine, 
the vertebral bodies are the most common site of metastasis. The 
cancers that frequently metastasize to the spine include those of 
the breast, lung, prostate, and kidney. 44 Epidural tumors are the 
most common cause of spinal cord injury (SCI) in cancer survi -
vors. Leptomeningeal metastases occur in a small percentage of 
cancer survivors but can cause significant neurologic dysfunction. 
Leukemia, lymphoma, melanoma, breast, and lung cancers are 
the most common types that metastasize to the leptomeninges. 35
SCI can result from cancer itself, its treatment, or other preexist-
ing or newly developed conditions. SCI can occur directly from the 
cancer, such as epidural, leptomeningeal, or intramedullary tumors, 
or indirectly from cancer treatment, such as radiation, chemother-
apy, or surgery. Infections, vascular disorders, degenerative disorders, 
and metabolic disorders can also cause SCI in patients with cancer.
The initial symptoms of spinal tumors differ greatly based on 
their location, size, and the extent of spread. Intramedullary  
tumors often present with motor/sensory abnormalities and 
symptoms of myelopathy. Whether inside or outside the spinal 
cord, tumors can disrupt neural elements and pathways, resulting 
in both local and distant effects. Neurologic dysfunction is related 
to disruption of spinal cord pathways, leading to sensory changes, 
muscle weakness, difficulty in walking, and abnormalities in 
bowel, bladder, and sexual functioning. Metastatic epidural spinal 
cord compression is a medical emergency. Magnetic resonance 
imaging is typically the imaging modality of choice. This requires 
swift diagnosis and treatment, which may include corticosteroids, 
radiation therapy, and surgery.30 Pain only during movement may 
indicate spinal instability, benefiting from surgical evaluation.63
Individuals with a neoplastic SCI benefit from comprehensive 
rehabilitation services. Although life expectancy varies, the median 
survival for individuals with a neoplastic SCI was just over 4 months 
after the date of admission to rehabilitation.61 Expected functional 
recovery for neoplastic SCI tends to be less than that of nonneoplastic 
SCI. Acute SCI patient care may include managing pain, autonomic 
dysreflexia, spasticity, neurogenic bowel and bladder dysfunction, 
and ventilation for those with high cervical level injuries. There is 
also an increased risk of deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, 
infections, and pressure injuries.80,182
Bone Metastasis
Bone is the most common site for metastatic spread. Cancers that 
commonly metastasize to the bone include lung, breast, prostate, 
thyroid, renal cell, lymphoma, myeloma, and melanoma. T reat -
ment of bone metastases may include medications, radiation 
therapy, and/or surgical stabilization depending on lesion loca -
tion, pain, risk of fracture, and neurologic compromise as well as 
the patient overall prognosis and medical comorbidities. Most 
unfractured lesions are managed with systemic antineoplastic 
therapy or radiation therapy.94
Bisphosphonates are utilized to decrease the progression of 
bone metastases and alleviate associated pain. Furthermore, 
bisphosphonates reduce the risk of fractures and hypercalcemia.133 
Radiation therapy offers an effective means of prompt local control 
of pain and tumor growth in bone metastases. Palliative radiation 
doses and protocols to bone lesions vary. Postsurgery radiation may 
be delayed but remains an adjunctive treatment to suppress tumor 
growth.47
The greatest physiatric concerns related to bony metastases are 
when those include lesions in the spine and long bones. These 
structures are crucial for weight bearing and mobility and are at 
risk of fracture. Painful osteolytic lesions are the lesions most 
prone to fracture. The clinical management of bone metastases at 
risk of fracture continues to be a challenge due to the difficulties 
in accurately determining the risk of fracture. Proposed rating 
scales include Mirels criteria for long bone lesions and spinal  
instability neoplastic score (SINS) for spine lesion. Details for 
scoring are outlined in Tables 30.9 and 30.10. For Mirels criteria, 
a score greater than 9 suggests need for potential surgical fixa -
tion.99,110 A SINS score of 0 to 6 indicates a more stable lesions, 
while 7 to 12 is potentially unstable, and greater than 13 is  
unstable. Surgical consultation is recommended for those with a 
score of 7 or above.50 In both risk assessments, functional pain is 
a significant predictor of potential pathologic fracture. However, 
an important note is that lesions are dynamic, and there may be 
tumor progression, which alters risk. For individuals with stable 
spines, treatment options may encompass oral and/or interven -
tional pain management, physical therapies, and bracing. The 
goals of management of pathologic fractures include alleviating 
pain, maintaining or restoring function, stabilizing the bone, and 
controlling the local tumor. 180 Surgery is considered if the  
patient’s life expectancy is more than 1 month for fractures in 
weight-bearing bones and more than 3 months for fractures in 
nonweight-bearing bones.180
Precautions in Rehabilitation
Cancer survivors benefit from rehabilitation interventions; however, 
due to the medical complexity and impacts from tumor and/or 
treatments, they may require additional precautions to optimize 
safety during rehabilitation interventions.
Precautions should be taken for individuals with peripheral 
neuropathy, and these recommendations will be tailored to the 
individual patient and the severity of their condition. It is essential 
to remember that CIPN can fluctuate during treatment, often 
worsening with additional chemotherapy and improving with dose 
reduction or treatment completion or discontinuation. Neuropa-
thy generally tends to be more sensory than motor, resulting in 
sensory ataxia rather than motor weakness, although both can  
occur. Balance training should be considered, along with aerobic 
alternatives, such as a stationary bike. Assistive devices and ortho-
ses may be beneficial. Resistance exercises should also be consid -
ered, with a focus on safety. The hands should be monitored with 
the consideration of using gloves. Exercise machines may be safer 
than free weights. Given the increased risk of falls, gait and balance

Myelosuppression is a common side effect associated with 
chemotherapy, immunosuppressive agents, and corticosteroids 
and necessitates precautions. Rehabilitation services are crucial for 
this population, and studies suggest that exercise not only  
improves physical performance but also reduces the duration of 
anemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, and the length of hospi-
talization.
41 However, anemia can reduce exercise tolerance and 
endurance, resulting in fatigue, dizziness, and hemodynamic  
instability. A hemoglobin level of 8 g/dL has previously been used 
as a threshold for concern about exercise. However, the stability 
of the anemia and any recent drop in hemoglobin should also be 
taken into account. Rather than using fixed hemoglobin param-
eters to determine safe participation in rehabilitation therapies, it 
is recommended to adjust therapeutic activity based on patient 
symptoms such as dyspnea, lightheadedness, and chest pain.
99 
Despite neutropenia, patients are encouraged to participate in 
therapy. There is no compelling evidence that rehabilitation inter-
ventions are contraindicated due to neutropenia. However, special 
consideration should be given to those experiencing side effects 
such as fatigue, malaise, dizziness, or lethargy. Infection precau -
tions such as reverse isolation, avoiding public gyms and pools 
with a preference for home-based exercise, and practicing hand 
hygiene with antimicrobial products during every patient encounter 
are recommended.
123 The primary concern with thrombocytopenia 
is the increased risk of bleeding, which is especially important to 
consider for those undergoing active chemotherapy or receiving a 
bone marrow transplant. In addition to the absolute number of 
platelets, it is crucial to determine if the thrombocytopenia is 
relatively stable or if the platelet count is actively declining. There 
is some evidence that actively falling counts can be more concern-
ing. Therefore it is important to collaborate closely with oncolo -
gists in managing this condition. General recommendations 
based on platelet counts are listed in Box 30.5.
112
When undergoing physical therapy, individuals with an ostomy 
need to take certain precautions to avoid complications such as a 
parastomal hernia. When performing resistance exercises, it is rec-
ommended to start with low intensity and gradually increase. The 
Valsalva maneuver, which involves holding one’s breath and strain-
ing during a strenuous activity, should be avoided because it can 
increase intraabdominal pressure. Therapists should ensure that 
patients maintain appropriate form and technique during exercises 
to avoid creating unnecessary pressure on the abdomen. Core  
exercises may need to be modified to prevent excessive intra -
abdominal pressure. Individuals with an ileostomy are at an increased 
risk of dehydration, so it is crucial to stay hydrated before, during, 
and after exercise. Additionally, emptying the ostomy bag prior

Bone metastasis and risk stratification assessments were previ -
ously discussed. Additional important considerations during 
physical therapy include precautions to avoid injury, especially  
in areas that are already compromised. High-impact activities, 
hyperflexion, hyperextension, and dynamic twisting should be 
avoided because they may put undue stress on affected sites.  
Exercise routines and guidance should be modified accordingly to 
account for these limitations. T o alleviate pressure on the affected 
limb, weight-bearing activities can be offloaded using an assistive 
device. Fall prevention is crucial in therapy, and safety education 
is necessary, particularly regarding activities that require lifting to 
prevent fractures during activities of daily living. Functional pain 
should be regarded with caution because it can indicate a risk of 
fracture. Thus the patient should be educated about the impor -
tance of reporting discomfort immediately to the therapist.
15,99
Modalities including heat, cryotherapy, transcutaneous electri-
cal nerve stimulation, and manual therapy can be utilized to help 
decrease pain. The cautions and contraindications are listed in 
Table 30.11, and these generally pertain to use directly over the 
tumor. Of note during end of life in the palliative care setting, 
their therapeutic modalities can be considered even in light of 
contraindications based on risk/benefit shared decision making 
based on the individuals’ goals.
99,153
Summary
Cancer rehabilitation is an essential service that enables cancer 
survivors to achieve and sustain the highest possible levels of func-
tion.
160 Currently, cancer rehabilitation care and the providers 
available vary widely.22 For a truly holistic approach that addresses 
most functional issues, the rehabilitation team should ideally  
include physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech-language 
pathology, neuropsychology, cognitive rehabilitation, lymphedema 
therapy, pelvic floor therapy, and physical medicine and rehabilita-
tion (physiatry). However, the reality is that this comprehensive 
team of specialists is seldom available to the survivors who require 
them. In fact, many patients face difficulties in accessing even a 
single proficient and informed practitioner, regardless of their spe-
cific needs.
157 The availability of effective treatment options and 
increase in survival rates highlight the importance of, value of, and 
need for more rehabilitation professionals to optimize and main -
tain cancer survivors’ function and quality of life. Although there 
is substantial evidence supporting the role of cancer rehabilitation 
in enhancing function and quality of life across numerous condi -
tions, there remains a substantial gap in high-level research to 
support this field’s interventions.
